era_context: 1646 marked the conclusion of the First English Civil War and the beginning of a period of political instability in England. The Thirty Years' War was in its final stages in Europe. The Qing Dynasty was consolidating its conquest of China. Global maritime competition was intensifying, with European powers competing for dominance in colonial territories.

events.title: End of the First English Civil War
events.description: The First English Civil War (1642–1646) concluded in 1646 with the decisive victory of the New Model Army and Parliament over the Royalist forces. The final major engagement was the Battle of Naseby (June 1645), but the war continued into 1646 with the surrender of the last Royalist strongholds. King Charles I surrendered to the Scottish army in May 1646 and was subsequently handed over to Parliament. The parliamentary victory established the dominance of the New Model Army and set the stage for the political crisis that would lead to the trial and execution of Charles I in 1649.

sources.name: Clarendon, Edward Hyde, Earl of. History of the Rebellion

events.certainty_note: The conclusion of the First English Civil War is documented in contemporary military and parliamentary records. The dates and major events are confirmed.

events.title: Surrender of King Charles I
events.description: In May 1646, King Charles I surrendered to the Scottish Covenanter army at Newark. This marked the effective end of his military resistance. Charles was subsequently handed over to Parliament in February 1647 in exchange for payment of the Scottish army. His surrender and subsequent captivity set the stage for the political crisis that would dominate English politics for the next three years, culminating in his trial and execution in 1649.
events.certainty_note: Charles I's surrender is documented in contemporary accounts and parliamentary records. The date and circumstances are confirmed.

events.title: Thirty Years' War Continuation and Peace Negotiations
events.description: In 1646, the Thirty Years' War continued despite ongoing peace negotiations at Münster and Osnabrück. Major military campaigns were still being conducted, particularly by French and Swedish forces against the Holy Roman Empire. However, the war was clearly winding down, with all major powers recognizing the need for peace. The negotiations were complex, involving disputes over territorial claims, religious toleration, and the balance of power. The war would officially end with the Treaty of Westphalia in October 1648.
events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Thirty Years' War and the ongoing peace negotiations are documented in contemporary diplomatic and military records.

events.title: Dutch-Portuguese Naval Conflict Continuation
events.description: In 1646, the Dutch West India Company (WIC) continued its conflict with Portuguese colonial forces in Brazil and the Atlantic. The Dutch had captured Pernambuco in 1630 and were consolidating control over Brazilian sugar production. Portuguese forces, supported by the Portuguese Restoration (1640), were attempting to reclaim lost territories. The conflict would continue until 1654, when the Portuguese would regain control of Brazil. This naval and colonial conflict reflected the broader struggle for dominance in the Atlantic world.
events.certainty_note: The broader conflict is well-documented, but specific 1646 engagements are less precisely dated. The year is assigned by convention within the larger campaign.

events.title: Qing Dynasty Consolidation in China
events.description: In 1646, the Qing Dynasty continued its consolidation following the 1644 conquest. The Shunzhi Emperor (age 3 in 1646) reigned under the regency of Dorgon. The Qing was systematically integrating Han Chinese administrative systems, establishing the examination system for bureaucratic recruitment, and consolidating control over the Han Chinese majority. Ming loyalist resistance was being suppressed, and the Qing was establishing the institutional foundations of its rule.
events.certainty_note: The broader consolidation process is well-documented, but specific 1646 events are less precisely dated. The year is assigned by convention.

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of the First English Civil War's conclusion as a clear parliamentary victory has been subject to modern historical revision. Some historians emphasize that the victory was primarily military (the New Model Army's victory) rather than parliamentary, and that Parliament itself was deeply divided. The extent to which the war's conclusion represented a decisive shift in power toward Parliament versus the military is debated. Additionally, some historians argue that Charles I's surrender was not inevitable and that alternative political settlements were possible in 1646.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1646 is exceptionally rich for English and European events. Military records, parliamentary documents, and contemporary chronicles provide detailed accounts. However, the perspective is heavily weighted toward English and Western European viewpoints. Eastern European and Asian events are less thoroughly documented in Western sources. Chinese sources are abundant but often filtered through later compilations. The historical record reflects the biases of 17th-century literacy: political and military events are well-documented, while social and economic history is sparse.

graph_edges.note: The parliamentary victory in the First English Civil War led to Charles I's surrender.
Charles I's captivity and the political crisis it created led to the New Model Army's ascendancy in 1647.
The continuation of the Thirty Years' War in 1646 led to the Treaty of Westphalia in 1648.
